food_subgroup: Chinese sweet soup dessert (tong sui) featuring peach gum (peach tree resin)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn term exists for peach gum dessert. FOODON_03301710 (dessert food product) is the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (sweet soups and puddings) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for peach gum tong sui.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for peach gum dessert or tong sui category.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 200 g (one bowl), which is typical for a Chinese sweet soup dessert portion served in Singapore tong sui shops.

nutrient_source.title: Peach Gum (Prunus persica resin) nutritional composition — estimated from USDA FoodData Central gelatin-based dessert analogue and published compositional data for peach gum polysaccharides
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy) and peer-reviewed compositional literature on Prunus persica resin
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for peach gum tong sui exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or ICMR-NIN. Nutritional values are estimated from: (1) published compositional studies on peach gum polysaccharides reporting ~85–90% carbohydrate (predominantly galactose-based polysaccharides) on a dry-weight basis; (2) USDA SR Legacy gelatin dessert analogues for the prepared sweet soup matrix; (3) rock sugar contribution for sugar_g and energy. The prepared dessert is approximately 85–90% water by weight. Per-100g values reflect the ready-to-eat soft-boiled dessert as served. Confidence is medium due to absence of a direct approved-source entry.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index estimated from analogous gelatinous polysaccharide-based Chinese sweet dessert; reference anchor: sweetened red bean soup GI ~43 and rock sugar syrup contribution; University of Sydney GI Database analogous food category
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for peach gum tong sui in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. GI is estimated at 45 (low category) based on: (1) the dominant carbohydrate fraction being high-molecular-weight galactose-based polysaccharides from peach gum, which are slowly digested and have a low glycemic impact analogous to other gum polysaccharides; (2) rock sugar (sucrose) contribution moderated by the polysaccharide matrix; (3) analogous Chinese sweet soups (e.g., red bean soup GI ~43, barley water GI ~25) from the University of Sydney GI Database. GL per 200g serving = (45 × 12.5 × 2.0) / 100 = 11.25, rounded to 11.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (~5 mg per 100g)
hypertension.guidance: Peach gum dessert is very low in sodium (~10 mg per 200g serving) and contains no added salt, making it a suitable occasional dessert choice for individuals managing hypertension, provided added sugar intake is monitored.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate sugar content from rock sugar (~10g sugar per 100g) with low GI (~45) due to polysaccharide matrix
type2_diabetes.guidance: Although peach gum has a low estimated glycemic index (~45) owing to its high-molecular-weight polysaccharide content, the added rock sugar contributes approximately 20g of sugar per standard 200g serving, and individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size and frequency, or request reduced-sugar preparation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible fat and saturated fat content; no cholesterol
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Peach gum dessert contains negligible fat (<0.2g per 100g), no saturated fat of clinical significance, and no cholesterol, making it a low cardiovascular-risk dessert option when consumed in moderate portions without excessive added sugar.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Soft-boiled peach gum dessert (桃胶糖水, Táo Jiāo Táng Shuǐ) is a traditional Chinese sweet soup (tong sui) made from peach gum — the natural resin secreted by Prunus persica (peach) trees — slow-boiled with rock sugar and water until the granules become translucent, gelatinous, and tender. Peach gum is harvested as amber-coloured crystalline nodules that must be soaked in water for 8–12 hours before cooking to expand and soften. Once cooked, the dessert has a smooth, silky, jelly-like texture with a mild, lightly sweet flavour. It is commonly served warm or chilled in Singapore tong sui shops and Chinese dessert establishments. Nutritionally, peach gum is predominantly composed of high-molecular-weight galactose-based polysaccharides, contributing dietary fibre and a low glycemic index (~45) despite the presence of added rock sugar. Per 100g of the prepared dessert, it provides approximately 52 kcal, 12.5g carbohydrate, 10g sugar, 0.8g dietary fibre, 0.3g protein, and 0.1g fat. The dessert is naturally low in sodium, fat-free in practical terms, and contains no cholesterol. It is suitable for vegetarians and vegans. Clinically, the low GI and negligible fat content make it a relatively benign dessert for cardiovascular health, though the added sugar warrants moderation for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The product description notes it is not suitable for infants, pregnant women, or breastfeeding mothers, consistent with traditional Chinese dietary cautions around peach gum. Grade-A peach gum refers to large, clean, amber-coloured granules with minimal impurities, considered the premium quality tier.
common_user_queries: How many calories in peach gum tong sui?
Is peach gum dessert suitable for diabetics?
What is the GI of peach gum dessert?
What does peach gum taste like?
Is peach gum the same as snow fungus?
Can pregnant women eat peach gum?

confidence_reason: No direct entry for peach gum tong sui exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, ICMR-NIN, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutritional values are estimated from published compositional studies on peach gum polysaccharides and USDA SR Legacy gelatin-based dessert analogues. GI is an analogous estimate anchored to University of Sydney GI Database comparable foods. Confidence is medium rather than low because the estimation methodology is transparent, the polysaccharide composition of peach gum is well-characterised in peer-reviewed literature, and the prepared dessert matrix is straightforward.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated for the ready-to-eat soft-boiled peach gum dessert as served (approximately 85–90% water by weight).
